Q1: The classification of studies into a 5-level hierarchy based on what remained stable at each level.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'studies fall into two groups: those where something remained stable across levels, and those where nothing did.'
Q3: A classification scheme was applied to prior studies, separating them into two groups based on what remained stable.

Q1: The generation of multiple sequences and checking whether the same four features appear in all.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'the same four features appear regardless of the order in which they are derived.'
Q3: Multiple sequences were generated and the same four features appeared in all of them.

Q1: The classification of audits by whether collapse occurred, whether tension was maintained, and explanatory gain.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'maintaining multiple descriptions simultaneously correlates with higher explanatory gain.'
Q3: Audits were classified by whether descriptions collapsed or were maintained, and gain was compared.

Q1: The classification of comparisons as viewpoint or claim type and measurement of collapse rates.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'comparisons where different perspectives on the same thing were compared did not collapse, while comparisons of competing answers did.'
Q3: Comparisons were classified by type and collapse rates were measured.

Q1: The generation of one world, representation in multiple ways, and application of the same detectors.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'some features appear regardless of how we represent the world, while others depend on the representation.'
Q3: One world was represented in multiple ways and the same detectors were applied to each.

Q1: The removal of each junction and checking whether the others remain meaningful.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'the four features form a chain where each depends on the previous one.'
Q3: Each junction was removed and the effect on the others was measured.

Q1: The systematic variation of removal fraction and measurement of recovery metrics.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'recovery metrics scale systematically with the magnitude of removal.'
Q3: Removal fraction was varied and recovery metrics were measured.

Q1: The variation of box width and measurement of packing structure and outcome variable.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'changing the container size changed the packing structure but did not predict the outcome.'
Q3: Box width was varied and the effect on packing structure and outcome was measured.

Q1: The expression of each junction as an operator and checking whether they can be composed from each other.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'all four features can be generated from a single feature plus composition rules.'
Q3: Each junction was expressed as an operator and composition was tested.

Q1: The re-reading of the study sequence using an interaction lens and recording whether gain appeared before or after interaction.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'explanatory gain appeared after interactions were available, not before.'
Q3: The study sequence was re-read and the timing of gain relative to interaction was recorded.

Q1: The generation of universes with only a continuous transformation operator and measurement of clustering.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'separable clusters emerged from continuous dynamics when nonlinearity and rotation were present.'
Q3: Universes were generated with continuous dynamics and the emergence of separable clusters was measured.

Q1: The generation of universes, application of detectors, and computation of temporal ordering.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'detectors form a layered temporal order with some appearing reliably before others.'
Q3: Universes were generated and detectors were applied to determine which appeared first.

Q1: The sweeping of removal fractions and measurement of recovery metrics.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'recovery metrics scale with removal fraction.'
Q3: Removal fraction was varied and recovery metrics were measured.

Q1: The analysis of universes with proximity detectors and testing of the chain bundling -> persistence -> categories.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'bundling appears, persistence appears less often, but categories never appear.'
Q3: Universes were analyzed for proximity structure and the chain was tested.

Q1: The measurement of pre-perturbation level and testing whether it predicts recovery.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'higher pre-perturbation values predict recovery after disruption.'
Q3: Pre-perturbation level was measured and its predictive power for recovery was tested.

Q1: The generation of worlds with no predefined pattern vocabulary and post-hoc detection of patterns.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'transitions occur in most worlds and certain patterns appear frequently.'
Q3: Worlds were generated without pattern vocabulary and patterns were detected after evolution.

Q1: The building of a co-occurrence matrix, subtraction of baseline, and spectral clustering.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'community structure can be recovered from interaction events by filtering noise and clustering.'
Q3: A co-occurrence matrix was built, noise was filtered, and clustering was applied.

Q1: The testing of reconstruction under four degradation conditions.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'reconstruction works under moderate degradation but breaks down at high levels.'
Q3: Reconstruction was tested under four degradation conditions and breakpoints were identified.

Q1: The testing of 10 candidate concepts for whether they require separation.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'all candidates required separation when stated or tested.'
Q3: Ten candidates were tested and all required separation.

Q1: The decomposition of audits into falsifications vs. genuine successes.
Q2: Yes - the result could be described as 'the simpler combination produced gain through productive failure, while the full combination required all components for new structure.'
Q3: Audits were decomposed by type and the contribution of each component was analyzed.
